coredata.dc:description: Good adhesive strength and joint durability are necessary for aluminum (Al)/polymer laminated film for lithium-ion battery packages. A modified propylene-based elastomer (mPBE) was prepared as the inner adhesive in this study. The polar group was grafted onto the PBE molecular chain via melt grafting with the assistance of styrene. The adhesive performance of PBE modified with glycidyl methacrylate is significantly higher than that of commercially available inner adhesives, Mitsui A and Dupont 739. Moreover, environmentally friendly trivalent chromium [Cr(III)] chemical conversion was used to treat Al foil and improve the adhesive joint. The wettability behavior of adhesive melt on the surface of Al foil was studied using high-temperature contact angle testing. The results indicate that wettability is not the sole factor impacting interfacial strength, surface energy of the adhesive melt also plays a role. To further study the effect of polar groups on the mPBE adhesion, a solvent casting-evaporation method was developed to prepare an ultra-thin mPBE coating on the Al foil surface. The morphology of mPBE-coated Al foils and the thickness of the coating were studied by atomic force microscope. The bonding mechanism at the interface was studied through time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectroscopy, and it was shown that the Cr(III) conversion coating could react with polar groups via acid-base interaction. Moreover, quantum chemical calculations based on density functional theory were used to reveal the bonding mechanism at the molecular level, and it is found that the adhesive strength is related to the electron-donor abilities and reactivity of the adhesive molecules.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Good adhesive strength and joint durability are necessary for aluminum (Al)/polymer laminated film for lithium-ion battery packages. A modified propylene-based elastomer (mPBE) was prepared as the inner adhesive in this study. The polar group was grafted onto the PBE molecular chain via melt grafting with the assistance of styrene. The adhesive performance of PBE modified with glycidyl methacrylate is significantly higher than that of commercially available inner adhesives, Mitsui A and Dupont 739. Moreover, environmentally friendly trivalent chromium [Cr(III)] chemical conversion was used to treat Al foil and improve the adhesive joint. The wettability behavior of adhesive melt on the surface of Al foil was studied using high-temperature contact angle testing. The results indicate that wettability is not the sole factor impacting interfacial strength, surface energy of the adhesive melt also plays a role. To further study the effect of polar groups on the mPBE adhesion, a solvent casting-evaporation method was developed to prepare an ultra-thin mPBE coating on the Al foil surface. The morphology of mPBE-coated Al foils and the thickness of the coating were studied by atomic force microscope. The bonding mechanism at the interface was studied through time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectroscopy, and it was shown that the Cr(III) conversion coating could react with polar groups via acid-base interaction. Moreover, quantum chemical calculations based on density functional theory were used to reveal the bonding mechanism at the molecular level, and it is found that the adhesive strength is related to the electron-donor abilities and reactivity of the adhesive molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer-metal hybrid composites have found extensive application in various industries such as automotive, packaging, electronics and others [1,2]. Polymer-metal hybrid composites take full advantage of the respective characteristics of polymers and metals. Aluminum (Al)/polymer laminated film as a package for soft package lithium-ion batteries (LIB) has been widely studied recently [3–5]. Al/polymer laminated film has a low density, which significantly reduces the weight of the battery. This makes the weight of soft package LIB lighter compared to steel and aluminum shell lithium batteries of the same capacity. Consequently, soft package LIB is lighter than steel shell and aluminum shell lithium batteries of equal capacity. Good formability makes it could be designed into batteries of any shape and size, thereby reducing the internal gaps of the battery and improving the volume utilization and capacity of LIB. Fig. 1 illustrates the structure of Al/polymer laminated film. Good adhesion of the inner adhesive to Al foil and joint durability in electrolytes are necessary for the Al/polymer laminated film. The inner adhesive layer is a modified propylene-based elastomer (PBE), which binds the Al/CPP layer and enhances the flexibility and impact resistance of Al/polymer film. Achieving a joint of high quality is a complicated issue because of the significant difference in chemical properties of the inner adhesive and Al foil. Therefore, it is essential to study bonding mechanisms fundamentally to optimize joint strength.

Several widely well-known theories clarifying the adhesive phenomena of polymer/metal interface have been established in the past years [6,7]. He et al. [8] investigated the wettability and adhesion of epoxy on the Ti alloy treated with different methods through contact angle measurements. Wu et al. [9] investigated the influence of different pre-treatment methods on the adhesion properties of nickel-plated layers on the surface of CFRP composite materials. The results showed that the pre-treatment could enhance the mechanical interlocking by increasing the roughness of the CFRP surface, thus improving the interfacial interaction and adhesion. The theory of chemical bonds is widely recognized and comprehensively studied among all theories [10]. The interfacial forces between polymer and metal include secondary bonds, acid-base interactions and primary bonds. Gensch et al. [11] revealed the interaction of poly(methyl methacrylate) derivative and Al by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The results indicated the formation of a new Al–O–C bond at the Al/polymer interface. Liu et al. [12] studied the interfacial chemistry in polyamide 66 (PA66)/Al alloy joints by using XPS. The formation of Al–O–C bonds was proven by XPS peaks for Al 2p and C 1s. Another characterization technique with greater spatial resolution and sensitivity compared to XPS is time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectroscopy (TOF-SIMS). To study the chemical interactions buried at the polymer/metal interface, Liang et al. [13] exposed the surface of modified polypropylene (PP)/Al foil by peeling the samples. Thereafter, the presence of COOAl+ ions representing the high chemical interaction of the compounds on the Al with the carboxyl groups in modified PP was successfully demonstrated by TOF-SIMS. Similarly, Zou et al. [14] investigated the bonding mechanisms of ethylene acrylic acid/chromium (Cr)-coated steel by analyzing the fractured surface after peeling through TOF-SIMS. The TOF-SIMS results demonstrated the occurrence of interaction between carboxyl groups and Cr oxides. It is worth noting that the thickness of the residual polymer on the peeled surface after detachment cannot be controlled. The thickness of the residual polymer on the substrate surface can even reach several millimeters (several thousand micrometers). The detection depth of TOF-SIMS is within 2 nm [15,16]. Moreover, TOF-SIMS could be employed to remove the surface layer by ion sputtering. However, the rate of ion sputtering is slow, measuring less than 1 μm/h, which may result in minor damage to the sample. Ion sputtering requires a long time to reach the adhesive/metal interface. Therefore, the requirements for sample preparation using the peeling method are highly stringent in order to obtain accurate information about the peeled surface. Therefore, a novel approach, namely the solvent-casting evaporation method, was developed to deposit ultra-thin adhesive coatings on the surface of the substrate in this study. The application of the solvent-casting evaporation method enables the rapid and precise detection of chemical information at the interface of the adhesive and substrate using TOF-SIMS.

To further investigate the molecular/atomic-level details of interfacial interactions between adhesive molecules and metals interface, which are challenging to obtain experimentally, computational simulation techniques were employed [17,18]. Quantum chemical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) can provide molecular-level information at the buried interface [19,20]. The calculated parameters related to the interaction include the molecular orbital energy (ELUMO and EHOMO), the dipole moment (
μ
) and the electron charge transfer (
Δ
N). The parameters could serve as an indicator to evaluate the relative strength of adsorption of a given molecule or molecular form on the metal [21,22]. A larger EHOMO value indicates a greater molecular ability to donate electrons. Conversely, a lower ELUMO value reflects the greater ability to accept electrons. In regards to molecule polarity, those with a higher dipole moment tend to participate in greater electrostatic interactions with other molecules. The parameter ΔN was utilized for either predicting the molecule-substrate interaction strength or elucidating the chemisorption energy trends.

In this study, oxygen-containing polar group, such as acrylic acid (AA), glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) and maleic anhydride (MAH), was grafted onto the PBE molecular chain via melt grafting with the assistance of styrene to improve the reactivity of nonpolar PBE. Moreover, environmentally friendly trivalent chromium [Cr(III)] chemical conversion was used to improve the physical and chemical properties of the Al foil to enhance the adhesive joint strength. The wettability and work adhesion of mPBE melts on the Al foil surface were analyzed by contact angle measurements at high temperatures. To further study the effect of polar groups on the mPBE adhesion and clarify the bonding mechanisms from a macro perspective to a micro perspective, the utilization of a solvent casting-evaporation method to prepare an ultra-thin mPBE coating on the surface of Cr-treated Al foil. The morphology of mPBE-coated Al foils and the thickness of the coatings were studied by atomic force microscope. The interactions buried at the adhesive and Al interface were characterized by TOF-SIMS. The correlation between adhesion performance dependence and quantum chemical parameters of adhesive molecules was investigated by quantum chemical calculations based on DFT.

Al foil (AA8021) is an Al–Fe alloy. The PBE resin, a thermoplastic elastomer, obtained from Exxon Mobil is a copolymer of propylene and ethylene. The chromic chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3
∙
6H2O), potassium hexacyanoferrate [K3Fe(CN)6] and phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 wt%) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium fluoride (NaF) and Styrene (St) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) was obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acrylic acid (AA) and maleic anhydride (MAH) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Mitsui A and Dupont 739 are oxygen functional group modified polyolefin resins purchased from Mitsui & Co., Ltd. (Japan) and Dupont Co., Ltd. (the United States), respectively.

The native Al foil (12 cm
×
18 cm
×
40
μ
m) underwent a degreasing process utilizing the solution composed of Na3PO4, Na2SiO3, Na2CO3 and NaF at 50 °C and then rinsed in deionized water, which was denoted “the degreased Al”. Then the degreased Al foil was subjected to immersion in a conversion solution of Cr(III) at ambient temperature for 2 min. The Cr(III) conversion solution containes 75 mM CrCl3
∙
6H2O, 50 mM NaF, 25 mM K3Fe(CN)6 and 150 mM H3PO4. Then the Cr(III)-treated Al foil was rinsed and dried at 50 °C, which was denoted “Cr–Al”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of inner adhesive
ce:section.ce:para: The polar groups were grafted on the PBE via the melt grafting reaction in a torque rheometer (Haake, Germany). As shown in Fig. 2, the blending process was conducted for 8 min at 180 °C. GMA, AA and MAH were used as grafted monomers. St was used to improve the grafting degree of grafted monomers. Dicumyl peroxide as an initiator was used to trigger the grafting reaction. The mass ratio of PBE/grafted monomer/initiator was 50/3.5/0.2. The molar ratio of grafted monomer/co-monomer was 1/1. The grafted specimens were solubilized in a heated xylene solvent and then dropped into acetone to obtain white products. The products were subjected to purification using acetone at 80 °C through Soxhlet extraction to remove the unreacted monomer. Then, the products were dried at 120 °C until a consistent weight was achieved. These samples were referred to as “MAH-PBE”, “GMA-PBE” and “AA-PBE”, respectively. The purification process was conducted as previously [23,24].

The films of mPBE after purification were obtained by compression-molding 1 g samples between poly(tetrafluoro ethylene). The process of hot-pressing was conducted utilizing a flat vulcanizer at 180 °C and 1 MPa for 2 min. FTIR spectra of mPBE were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. A total of 32 scans were carried out utilizing a resolution of 4 cm−1. The final infrared spectra were obtained by subtracting the peaks of carbon dioxide and water from the original spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface energy, creep compliance and wettability of the molten adhesives
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface energy of the molten adhesive was measured by Kruss K100C-MK2 according to the BS EN 14370:2004 (Wilhelmy plate method). The part of the platinum plate (2 cm
×
1 cm) immersed in the molten adhesive was 2 mm. The test temperature of the melt was 180 °C. The air density was 1.29 kg/m3. The melt density was measured by a melt flow rate meter (ZRZ1452, MTS) with 2.095 mm
±
0.005 mm inner diameter according to ISO 1133:1997. The chemical parameters of mPBEs are listed in Table 1.

The creep compliance was carried out on a MARS III rheometer. The application of a consistent force of 2.5 N on adhesive films that were positioned between two disposable rotors. The creep compliance of the films was measured over time (t) at a temperature of 180 °C. The radius of the specimen was 10.25 mm.

The adhesives were placed on the surface of the Al foil in the heating chamber at 180 °C and the wetting process of the adhesive melt on the substrate was recorded. The contact angle images were captured every 30 s by using Dataphysics OCA20 until the contact angles of the adhesive melt on the substrate remained constant. Three replicates of the experiment were performed for each sample and the average value of the results was taken.

mPBE was dissolved in xylene and stirred for 4 h at 150 °C to form mPBE solution of different concentrations. After the mPBE solution had cooled to room temperature, the mPBE solution was uniformly cast on the treated Al foil at room temperature using a 2 μm coating bar. The samples were oven-dried at 180 °C for 2 min. Excess unbounded mPBE was removed with pure xylene and the surface was dried again at 180 °C for 2 min. An ultra-thin mPBE coating was then deposited on the Al foil surface, and the prepared sample was stored in a vacuum-sealed self-sealing bag for subsequent characterizations. The buried hybrid interface was characterized using TOF-SIMS. The TOF-SIMS analysis was conducted utilizing the ION-TOF SIMS 5. The ionization source was the Bi+ primary ion beam. The acquisition of mass spectra was conducted through the utilization of a pulsed ion beam with a kinetic energy of 30 keV and a current of 1.00 pA. The scanning of the ion beam was performed on a sample area measuring 300 × 300 μm2.

The composition of Al foils and mPBE-coated Cr–Al were characterized by XPS. The XPS spectra were recorded using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+ with an Al Kα monochromatic X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The irradiation current was 15 mA, the voltage was 15 kV, and the vacuum was approximately 5 × 10−9 mbar in the analytical chamber. Survey scans were taken with a step size of 1.0 eV and a pass energy of 150.0 eV. High-resolution scans of O 1s, C 1s, Al 2p and Cr 2p were taken at a pass-energy of 50.0 eV and an energy step of 0.05 eV. The photoelectron take-off angle to the sample surface in the measurement was 60°. The spot size was 400 μm. After subtracting a Shirley background, the high-resolution XPS spectra of the above elements were fitted by using Gaussian/Lorentzian functions XPSPeak.

The SEM images were obtained by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, QUANTAX 400-30). The accelerating voltage was 15 kV and the emission current was 15 μA, respectively. Electron micrographs were acquired in the secondary electron imaging mode. All images had a resolution of 1024 pixels by 960 pixels.

An atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) was used to characterize the morphology and roughness of Al foils before and after Cr(III) conversion treatment. Moreover, the morphology of the coating deposited on the Cr–Al was also studied by AFM. Half of the surface of the Al foil was coated with mPBE, while the other half was not coated. The detection position of the AFM probe was at the boundary between the coated and non-coated surface of Al foil. Moreover, to obtain the thickness of the ultra-thin mPBE coating, mPBE coating was deposited on a mica sheet with extremely low roughness. Before depositing the mPBE coating, the mica sheet was immersed in acetone and subjected to ultrasonic cleaning for 10 min. The AFM was operated in the contact mode scanning. The spring constant of the AFM probe is 0.4 N/m with a resonance frequency of 13 kHz. AFM images were processed using the Bruker Nanoscope Analysis software.

Depth profile and Cr coating thickness were obtained by using an Auger Electron Spectrometer (AES, PHI 710). The vacuum degree of the chamber was 3 × 10−8 Pa. The e-beam acceleration voltage is 10 kV and the current of the e-beam with incident 30° from surface normal is 10 nA. Ion sputtering was performed with 4 kV Ar ions (emission current of 20 mA and Ar pressure of 4.3 mPa). Argon gas was used as the gas source. The test area is 300 μm
×
300 μm.

The adhesives were placed between polyimide sheets for preheating at 180 °C for 5 min. Then adhesive films were prepared by hot pressing at 180 °C and 10 MPa pressure for 20 s. To keep the content of polar groups in the adhesives consistent, the mass ratio of the adhesive used is calculated based on the grafting rate:

The T-peeling structure is depicted in Fig. 3
. The semi-finished film (SFF) is composed of nylon/outer adhesive/Al foil. The adhesive strength between Al foils and adhesive films was evaluated by laminating the adhesive films between two SFF films. The T-peeling experiments were conducted by employing a universal testing machine (E43, MTS) to separate the two SFF films at 5 mm/s following the ASTM D903. The equation used to determine the T-peeling strength was as follows:
3
G =
F W

The T-peeling strength, denoted by G, is a measure of the force required to separate two SFF films. The average force (N) needed for this separation is represented by F, while the width (mm) of the sample is denoted by W. The final result was obtained by taking the average value of five samples tested for each system.

The molecular structures of the smallest repeating unit of PBE containing one polar group and co-monomer were using first principle calculations. The geometries of these molecular structures were first optimized using PM6 semiempirical method [25]. The results from PM6 calculations went through further optimization via B3LYP hybrid functional with the 6-31G∗ basis set [26,27]. All these geometry optimizations were done in the gas phase. And no restrictions are applied to the optimization process.

The optimized structures were further calculated with the hybrid method of M06-2X [28] and Def2-TZVP basis set [29] to obtain the final global minimum energy structures. Quantum chemical calculations were conducted by using the Gaussian 09 software [30]. The ionization potential (IP) and the electron affinity (EA) were calculated using the method as follows [31].

The Condensed Fukui Function (CFF) was introduced to analyze the nucleophilic or electrophilic attack susceptibility sites of the adhesive molecules. The CFF aims to condense the Fukui Function onto all atoms, resulting in precise values for each individual atom. The quantitative analysis of atoms’ electron-donating or accepting abilities can be achieved by comparing the CFF value [33].

represent atoms with a neutral, positive and negative charge respectively. The orbital-weighted dual descriptor, based on the dual descriptor in Koopmans' approximation, was employed to provide a more intuitive description of the CFF values on each atom [34]. The MULTIWFN program was used to process the results of Gaussisan 09 software to obtain the CFF [35,36].

Fig. 4 represents the FTIR spectra of pure PBE, AA-PBE, GMA-PBE and MAH-PBE. Remarkable peaks in 1715–1862 cm−1 appear in the FTIR spectra of mPBEs. The characteristic peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 1735 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of carbonyl groups (CO) of carboxylic groups in the AA and CO of ester groups in the GMA, respectively [37,38]. The peaks at 910 cm−1 and 843 cm−1 observed in GMA-PBE are characteristic of the epoxy group in the GMA, relatively weak compared to the CO peak [39]. The band at 1862 cm−1 is due to the CO symmetric stretching in the MAH, whereas the band at 1787 cm−1 is attributed to the CO symmetric stretching in the MAH [24]. Moreover, the peaks around 1601 cm−1 and 705 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of mPBEs correspond to the characteristic of the benzene ring in the St. These results show that St has successfully facilitated the grafting of MAH, GMA, and AA onto PBE molecular chain.

The deformability of adhesives was determined by creep compliance under constant pressure at the bonding temperature. The stronger the adhesive deformability, the larger the established adhesive area between the adhesive and substrate [40,41]:
11
A
A o

e
− J
( t )
≃ J
( t )
where A and A
0 represent the established adhesive area and the contact area, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5 , curves of creep compliance J(t) of the adhesives over time allow the observation of the deformability changes of adhesives under constant force. It is seen that grafting polar groups onto PBE would reduce the deformability of PBE. This is because the increment in intermolecular force due to the polar groups on the mPBEs impedes the movement of the PBE chains. Moreover, the polarity of MAH is stronger than that of GMA and AA [42], resulting in stronger intermolecular forces in MAH-PBE. Thus, the creep compliance of MAH-PBE is the lowest. It is worth noting that the longer molecular chain of GMA compared to that of AA and MAH plays a role as a plasticizer. Thus, the creep compliance of GMA-PBE is higher than that of MAH-PBE and AA-PBE. This result illustrates that the established adhesive area of GMA-PBE is larger than that of MAH-PBE and AA-PBE under constant pressure at high temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and roughness of Al foils
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 6 , the strip-shaped roller marks on the Al foils are generated during the cold-rolling of Al foils. After Cr(III) conversion treatment, the surface of the degreased Al will generate many particles. According to the EDS results in Table 2
, these particles are oxides and phosphides of Cr deposited on the Al–Fe intermetallic particles on the surface of the Al foil. According to Fig. 7 , the surface roughness of the Cr–Al is significantly increased from 50.5 nm to 79.4 nm compared to the degreased Al, due to the deposition of Cr compounds and the exposure of Al–Fe particles on the surface of the Cr–Al.

The XPS full spectra and the atomic proportion of the main elements of Cr–Al and the degreased Al are illustrated in Fig. 8 A and Table 3 , respectively. The carbon on the Cr–Al is considered to be a contaminant deposited during exposure to ambient air. Compared to the degreased Al, the O/Al atom ratio of Cr–Al is higher. This result indicates that the content of oxygenated compounds exhibits an increase after Cr(III) treatment. Moreover, the content of O is the highest in film-forming elements, which suggests that oxygenated compounds are the main components of all Al foils. As shown in Fig. 8B, the binding energy of aluminum oxide is 73.2 eV, while that of aluminum hydroxide is 73.9 eV. The binding energy of boehmite (AlOOH) formed by alkaline treatment is 74.22 eV. 64 % of the Al3+ is in the form of Al2O3, 24 % is Al(OH)3, 12 % is AlOOH. On the one hand, Al2O3 has a lower solubility product compared to Al hydroxides, making it more prone to precipitation on the surface of Al foil during the alkaline treatment. On the other hand, the dehydration of Al hydroxides results in the formation of Al2O3 on the surface of the Al foil during the drying process. Therefore, the content of Al oxide is higher than that of Al hydroxide on the surface of the degreased Al.

The Cr 2p3/2 curve in Fig. 8C shows that three types of Cr compounds are formed on the Cr–Al. Among these, the Cr is mainly in the form of Cr2O3. 52 % (in the atom percent) of the Cr is in the form of Cr2O3, 25 % is Cr(OH)3 and the rest is CrPO4. As illustrated in Figs. 8D and 46 % of the Al3+ is in the form of Al2O3, 17 % is Al(OH)3 and the rest is AlPO4 and AlF3. The deposition procedure of Cr(III) conversion coating is as follows. In the initial stage, the Al oxide and hydroxides layer on degreased Al may be dissolved in the acid solution, which is accelerated by F− in the treatment solution until the Al substrate is exposed.
12

Upon dissolution of the Al substrate in acid, electron loss occurs, leading to oxidation.
13

On the Cr(III)-converted Al foil, the treatment solution does not contain Cr(VI) ions. Thus, no CrO3 is found on the Al surface. Cr(III) oxide and hydroxide are formed from the redox reaction at IMPs. An electrochemical cell is created at the interface between IMPs and Al substrate. The Al acts as an anodic site and attracts OH− ions from the solution, while the Fe acts as a cathodic site and attracts cations (H+, Al3+ and Cr3+) from the solution. The pH increases gradually due to the consumption of H+ and the generation of OH−. Then, the cations react with OH− around Al–Fe IMPs. Cr(III) oxide and hydroxide are formed [43,44]:
16

The consumption of H+ also accelerates the ionization of H3PO4. Chromium phosphate is formed when its solubility product is exceeded.

During the chemical conversion process, phosphoric acid and sodium fluoride dissolve the oxide layer, simultaneously allowing the deposition of Al compounds and chromium compounds mainly near the intermetallic compound particles, thereby exposing the Al substrate. This is evident from the EDS results, indicating a higher metallic content of Al on the surface of the Cr–Al. The depth profile of Cr(III) conversion coating on the Cr–Al is shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that the Cr is present in the near-surface area on the Cr–Al and the thickness of Cr(III) conversion coating is approximately 100 nm. Cr(III) conversion coating mainly consists of oxygen compounds, which is consistent with the XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wetting behavior and adhesion work of adhesive melts on the degreased Al and Cr–Al
ce:section.ce:para: Higher wettability leads to increased contact area between the adhesive liquid and solid surface, resulting in stronger interaction forces between the adhesive and the substrate. The wetting behavior of adhesive liquid depends on temperature, residence time and the surface energies of the substrate, liquid and interface. The wettability of various adhesive melts under conditions closer to bonding was investigated through contact angle measurement at 180 °C. Fig. 10 illustrates the variation in contact angles of adhesive melt on the Cr–Al substrate over time. The decreased contact angle of polymer melts over time could be categorized into three different stages. The first stage was the melting of the polymer. As shown in Fig. 11 , the cap and the foot of the molten droplets could be observed in the second stage. The presence of foot could be ascribed to the heterogeneous melting rate of polymer melts [45]. In the final stage, the contact angles decrease to a stable value. As shown in Fig. 12 , the contact angle of adhesive melts reaches a stable value after approximately 210 s.

As shown in Fig. 12, the wetting property of the adhesive melts on the Cr–Al surface is better than that on the degreased Al. This is because the surface of Cr–Al contains Cr2O3 with higher surface energy than Al2O3. Cr2O3 exhibits greater atomic instability on its surface compared to Al2O3, leading to reduced thermodynamic stability and increased surface energy [46,47]. In addition, according to the XPS results, it can be observed that the phosphate groups are only present on the Cr–Al and not on the degreased Al. The EDS results further indicate that the phosphate groups are not only present in the Cr(III) conversion coating but also on the Al foil surface not covered by the conversion coating. Compared to the degreased Al, the presence of phosphate groups on the Cr–Al can enhance the hydrophilicity of Al foil surfaces, leading to an increase in surface energy [48]. Young's equation illustrates the relationship between contact angle and solid-liquid surface energy [49]:
19
cos ⁡ θ =
σ S
−
σ
S L
σ L

The higher the surface energy of the substrate, the better the wetting property of the adhesive. For the same adhesive melt, the contact angle on the surface of the Cr–Al with high surface energy is smaller than on the surface of the degreased Al with low surface energy. Cr(III) conversion treatment improves the wettability of the adhesive melt on the surface of Al foil.

Compared to the pure PBE, mPBEs grafted with polar functional groups exhibit increased contact angles on the surfaces of degreased Al and Cr–Al. The graft modification seems to be detrimental to the wettability of the adhesive melt on the substrate surface. However, the work of adhesion (
W a
), referring to the energy required to separate adhesive and substrate, is related to the contact angle
θ
and surface energy of adhesive melts
σ L
[50].
20
W a
=
σ L
(
1 + cos
θ
)

As shown in Fig. 12, the wetting behavior of mPBE melts on the metal surface decreases due to the introduction of polar functional groups compared to PBE melt. However, the surface energy of mPBE melts simultaneously increases due to the presence of polar functional groups on the molecule chain. The results calculated by Eq. (20) in Fig. 13 indicate that mPBE has poorer wetting properties compared to PBE melts, but its higher surface energy will lead to a higher value of
W a
. The increment in
W a
of mPBEs compared to the pure PBE is mainly derived from the interaction between grafted polar groups and metal surfaces. The value of
W a
of GMA-PBE is the highest, indicating that the interaction between GMA and metal is stronger than other polar groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the ultrathin mPBEs/Cr–Al interface
ce:section.ce:para: AFM was used to study better the morphology of the coating deposited on the Cr–Al and the thickness of the coating. As shown in Fig. 14 A, a significant difference in morphology on both sides of the boundary can be observed when the xylene/mPBE solution concentration is 3 g/L. The rough coating covers the Cr–Al surface, along with the presence of polymer particles. As shown in Fig. 14B, the coating becomes smoother at a concentration of 0.3 g/L, and it is insufficient to fully cover the Cr–Al surface. As shown in Fig. 14C and D, the surface morphology of the Cr–Al with the deposited ultra-thin coating does not change significantly. This is because the deposited nanocoating is extremely thin compared to the high surface roughness of the Cr–Al. The thickness of the thin coating could not be measured on the rough surface of Cr–Al.

Therefore, the ultra-thin coating was deposited on a mica sheet with extremely low roughness to obtain the thickness of the ultra-thin coating. As shown in Fig. 15 A, when the xylene/mPBE solution concentration is 3 g/L, the morphology of the mPBE film deposited on the mica sheet is similar to that deposited on the Cr–Al surface, with rough surfaces and particles. The coating thickness is approximately tens of nanometers. Additionally, the holes on the film surface may be caused by the volatilization of xylene during high-temperature drying. As shown in Fig. 15B, the thickness of the deposited coating is about 20–40 nm at a solution concentration of 0.3 g/L. Similar to the morphology of mPBE-coated Cr–Al in Fig. 14B, the surface deposited coating on the mica sheet becomes smoother when the solution concentration decreases. As shown in Fig. 15C, the film-forming property of the deposited coating deteriorates as the solution concentration decreases to 0.03 g/L. The coating thickness ranges from several nanometers to a dozen nanometers when the concentration of the xylene/mPBE solution is 0.03 g/L. As shown in Fig. 15D, island structures are formed when the concentration is 0.003 g/L. The thickness of the coatings is approximately 1 nm in some areas, whereas in thicker regions, the thickness can even reach 4–5 nm. Moreover, a layer of ultrathin coating with a thickness in several angstroms seems to be deposited beneath the islands in the marked region.

The XPS full spectra and O 1s peaks of the Cr–Al with and without MAH-PBE coating obtained from different concentrations of mPBE/xylene solution are shown in Fig. 16 . The XPS full spectra show that the ultra-thin mPBE coating significantly increases the C content on the Cr–Al surface. The mPBE coatings on the Cr–Al obscure the signals of Cr(III) conversion coating, resulting in a significant attenuation in the O 1s and Cr 2p signals on the surface. Moreover, the O 1s peaks for Cr–Al deposited mPBE coatings shift to higher binding energies compared to the Cr–Al without mPBE coatings. This result indicates that mPBE coatings alter the chemical states in the O 1s on the Cr–Al and new structures appear.

TOF-SIMS mass spectra obtained from the ultrathin polymer overlayer on the Cr–Al are shown in Fig. 17 . The mass spectra of ultrathin mPBE coatings, obtained from 0.003 g/L solution, on the Cr–Al are similar to each other. It can be seen that the Al+ and Cr+ could still be detected for the thick MAH-PBE coating (thickness of more than 2 nm) obtained from 0.03 g/L solution. This is attributed to the non-uniform coverage of the coating, which is consistent with the AFM results. The kind of signal peaks of PBE coating is more than other coatings. This might be because the higher mPBE content on the Cr–Al compared to the PBE buries the information of these fragment ions.

The representative ion fragments obtained from the mass spectra of ultrathin mPBE coatings from a concentration of 0.003 g/L solution on the Cr–Al are shown in Fig. 18 . The newly formed products, CO2Cr+ and CO2Al+, are the result of interfacial interactions, such as Brønsted acid-base interaction and Lewis acid-base interaction. A similar result has been found in previous studies [13,51]. Compared to other samples, two special peaks, C2H3OAl+ and C2H3OCr+, are only observed at the GMA-PBE/Cr–Al interface. This result indicates that the epoxy groups of GMA could interact with metal oxides. The TOF-SIMS signal of the interface chemical bond might originate from the interface between coatings with a thickness of less than 2 nm and Cr–Al. A Schematic illustration of Lewis acid-base interactions and Brønsted acid-base interactions is shown in Fig. 19 .

The coordination bonds are formed through Lewis acid-base reactions between the lone pairs of electrons on oxygen atoms in esters, acid anhydrides, carboxyl groups, or epoxy groups and empty orbitals, including the p orbitals of Al3+ and the d orbitals of the Cr3+, in metal oxides. On the other hand, the ion bonds are formed through Brønsted acid-base reaction between carboxyl groups generated from MAH hydrolysis and AA carboxyl groups with metal hydroxides [52–54]. In a word, CO2Cr+ and CO2Al + could be generated through both Lewis acid-base reactions and Brønsted acid-base reactions.

Higher values of the dipole moment (
μ
) are associated with the greater polarity of the molecule. The polarity of MAH-PBE is the greatest among mPBE molecules. This might explain why MAH-PBE melt has the highest surface energy. After deprotonation, the corresponding anions show greater polarity compared to MAH-PBE and AA-PBE molecules. Quantum chemical parameters calculated from Eq. (4)–(10) are listed in Table 4 . The acid-base chemical reaction strength is often evaluated using ΔN [55].
21
Δ N =
(
χ substrate
−
χ adhesive
)
2
(
η substrate
+
η adhesive
)
Where
χ substrate
and
χ adhesive
represent electronegativity of metal and adhesive, respectively.
η substrate
and
η adhesive
are the hardness of metal and adhesive, respectively. If ΔN > 0, it indicates the flow of electrons from the adhesive to the substrate. Conversely, the electron flow is in the opposite direction if ΔN < 0 [56]. The electronegativity and global hardness of metal oxides bonded with mPBE molecules and metal cations bonded with mPBE anions are listed in Table 5 . The
Δ N
from the mPBE molecules and corresponding anions to the metal oxides and metal cations are displayed in Figs. 20 and 21
, respectively.

All the
Δ N
value is positive, indicating the flow of electrons from the adhesive to the Al substrate. A higher
Δ N
value will lead to better adsorption due to the stronger interaction at the interface [57]. As shown in Fig. 20, all mPBE molecules bind to Cr2O3 more tightly than Al2O3. Compared to other molecules, GMA-PBE has the highest
Δ N
value with metal oxides. This might be because of the greatest electron-donation ability and reactivity of GMA-PBE. When MAH-PBE and AA-PBE molecules interact with metal hydroxides on the Cr–Al, MAH-PBE anion and AA-PBE anion could bind to metal cations to form carboxylates. As shown in Fig. 21,
Δ N
values between MAH-PBE anion and AA-PBE anion and metal cations are significantly higher than that between the corresponding molecules and metal oxides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The molecular capacity to donate and accept electrons
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the influence of the electronic structure of polar groups, the optimized geometries of the smallest repeating unit of PBE containing one polar group and co-monomer with ELUMO, EHOMO and distributions are presented in Fig. 22 . As shown in Fig. 22, the electron-donation abilities represented by the EHOMO value increase in the following order: GMA-PBE > MAH-PBE > AA-PBE > PBE. Moreover, the electron-accepting abilities indicated by the ELUMO value increase in the following order: MAH-PBE > GMA-PBE > AA-PBE > PBE. This result indicates that GMA-PBE most easily donates electrons. The establishment of chemical bonds during the adhesive process is achieved through a reciprocal exchange of electrons between the adhesive molecule and the substrate. The distribution of HOMO orbitals on the whole polar groups, especially on the oxygen atoms, is favorable for the formation of chemical bonds. The distribution of HOMO orbitals transfers from the skeleton of the PBE chain to the grafted groups after grafting modification. The results illustrate that the grafted group on the PBE chain shows good capacity for electron donation.

The energy gap between EHOMO and ELUMO (ΔE) indicates the reactivity of the adhesive molecules reacting with the metal. The smaller the ΔE value is, the more reactive the molecule is [21]. The order of ΔE values is observed to be GMA-PBE < MAH-PBE < AA-PBE < PBE. This result indicates that GMA-PBE has the greatest reactivity when the molecule interacts with the metal surface.

The reactive sites of molecules and corresponding anions are shown in Fig. 23 . Green and blue areas (positive and negative values) represent the areas susceptible to electrophilic and nucleophilic attack, respectively. In other words, green and blue areas tend to accept and donate electrons, respectively. A larger green or blue area implies a larger CFF value and a more active corresponding area [60]. As shown in Fig. 23, the sites susceptible to nucleophilic attack transfer from the skeleton of the PBE chain to the oxygen atoms in the grafted groups after grafting modification. Thus, the oxygen atoms donating electrons could be coordinated to Al and Cr cations containing vacant orbitals in metal oxides through Lewis acid-base interaction when the mPBEs interact with metal oxides (Al2O3 and Cr2O3) on the Cr–Al. In this reaction, the oxygen atoms in the anhydride groups of MAH, the carboxyl groups of AA, the ester groups of GMA and the epoxy groups of GMA serve as Lewis base while the metal cations in the metal oxides as Lewis acid. This result is in accordance with the result of TOF-SIMS. Interestingly, in GMA, the oxygen atom of the epoxy group can only donate electrons but cannot receive electrons. This may be the reason why GMA has a strong electron-donating ability.

Different from GMA-PBE, MAH-PBE and AA-PBE would deprotonate to form carboxylate anions due to the presence of hydroxyls in metal hydroxides on the Cr-Al [61,62]. This process is analogous to the acid-base neutralization reaction. MAH and AA in this reaction. As shown in Fig. 23, the blue area on the carboxylate anions of the MAH-PBE anion and AA-PBE anion is larger than that on the corresponding molecule. This result indicates that the MAH-PBE anion and AA-PBE anion have a stronger nucleophilicity than the corresponding molecule.

Fig. 24 illustrates that the adhesion strength between pure PBE and Al foils is almost 0 N/mm. Polar groups on the mPBEs significantly improve the adhesive performance of PBE. The adhesive strengths between mPBEs and Cr–Al are higher than those between mPBEs and the degreased Al. The adhesive strength of GMA-PBE is the highest, reaching 3.9 N/mm on the Cr–Al. The adhesive strength of GMA-PBE with the Cr–Al is 83.1 % higher than that of Mitsui A and 21.1 % higher than that of Dupont 739, which are commercially available inner adhesives used in the Al/P film. The adhesive strength is almost 0 N/mm for pure PBE without grafted polar groups on both the degreased Al with low roughness and Cr–Al with high roughness. Moreover, the adhesive strength between GMA-PBE with reactive polar groups and the degreased Al with low roughness is significantly higher than that between pure PBE and Cr–Al with high roughness. These results suggest that roughness has a minimal impact on adhesion enhancement, whereas chemical bonding contributes significantly more to adhesion than mechanical interlocking. Cr(III) conversion treatment does not improve interface bonding by increasing roughness but rather by altering the surface chemical composition of the Al foil to enhance interfacial interaction and bonding.

GMA-PBE has the highest adhesive strength among mPBEs due to its superior electron-donation ability and reactivity. The primary components of Cr(III) conversion coating are metal oxides. Compared to AA-PBE and MAH-PBE, GMA-PBE has a higher
Δ N
value with metal oxides, especially Cr2O3. This results in the strongest chemical bonds being formed at the interface of GMA-PBE/Al foils. Moreover, the established adhesive area of GMA-PBE is larger than that of MAH-PBE and AA-PBE under constant pressure at high temperatures. Although the stronger bonds could be formed by MAH-PBE and AA-PBE with metal hydroxides via Brønsted acid-base interactions, the content of metal hydroxides on the Al foils is significantly less than that of metal oxides. The number of chemical bonds formed via Brønsted acid-base interactions is limited. Moreover, the chemisorbed water content also has an impact on Brønsted acid-base interactions. During the intermediate step, the presence of water is necessary for the dissociation of acid to carboxylate anions. A lower amount of water adsorption on a metal surface is less favorable for the formation of ionic bonds at the interface [54,63]. In a word, AA and MAH exhibit a strong Brønsted acid-base reaction with metal cations, whereas such interactions require more rigorous conditions than Lewis acid-base reactions. The interface reactions are dominated by Lewis acid-base interactions. Thus, AA-PBE and MAH-PBE have less adhesive strength than GMA-PBE on the Al foils in this study.

In this study, a modified propylene-based elastomer (mPBE) was prepared as the inner adhesive in the package for lithium-ion batteries. Oxygen-containing polar group, such as AA, GMA and MAH, was grafted onto the PBE molecular chain via melt grafting with the assistance of styrene. The adhesive performance of GMA-PBE is significantly higher than that of commercially available inner adhesives, Mitsui A and Dupont 739, by 83.1 % and 21.1 %, respectively. Moreover, Cr(III) conversion treatment does not improve interface bonding by increasing roughness but rather by altering the surface chemical composition of the Al foil to enhance interfacial interaction and bonding. Chemical bonding contributes significantly more to adhesion than mechanical interlocking. Moreover, the adhesive strength at the interface is associated with the wettability and surface energy of the adhesive melt. Graft modification has a negative impact on the wettability of adhesive melt on the substrate but simultaneously increases the surface energy of the adhesive melt, consequently resulting in improving adhesion work at the interface.

Moreover, the effect of polar groups on the adhesion of mPBEs was studied at a molecular level. TOF-SIMS analysis reveals the presence of newly formed products, suggesting an acid-base interaction between polar groups and the surface of Cr-treated Al treated. The results of quantum chemical parameters, such as ELUMO, EHOMO,
Δ
E, electronegativity and absolute hardness, of PBE and mPBEs indicate that the adhesive strength is related to the electron-donor abilities and reactivity of the mPBE molecules. The stronger electron-donor ability of GMA-PBE has a higher charge transfer fraction and stronger interfacial interactions with metal oxides. Moreover, the interfacial interactions at the joint of mPBEs and Al foils are mainly dominated by Lewis acid-base interactions. The present study provides guidance for exploring and comprehending the interaction mechanism in polymer-metal laminated composites.
